model_narrative: We run a small oil refinery. We buy crude oil of different types, run it through a set of refining processes to produce intermediate streams, optionally buy some intermediates from outside, then blend those intermediates into a handful of final products that we sell. We choose how much of each process to run on each crude, how much crude and how much external intermediate material to purchase, how to blend intermediates into each product, and how much of each product to sell. The goal is to maximize profit, which is sales revenue minus the cost of input materials minus the operating cost of the processes.

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the crude oil types that can be purchased and refined

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the refining processes that turn crude into intermediate materials

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the material components that appear in process yields, including crude itself, the intermediate streams, and the final products

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final blended products that are sold

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the intermediate blending materials produced by the processes and combined into final products

sets.name: cd
sets.doc: the subset of intermediate materials that may also be bought externally rather than only produced in house; this is a subset of the intermediate materials

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the capacity-limited processing units whose throughput is bounded

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the measurable quality attributes of a blended product, such as octane and sulfur content

sets.name: lim
sets.doc: the two directions a quality specification can take, a lower bound or an upper bound

sets.name: bp
sets.doc: the set of allowed pairs of a final product and an intermediate material; a pair belongs to this set exactly when that intermediate is permitted to be blended into that final product

params.name: a
params.doc: the yield coefficient of component c from crude cr in process p; a positive value means the process produces that component and a negative value means it consumes it, per unit of process run; defaults to zero when a component is not involved in a process

params.name: b
params.doc: the amount of capacity of processing unit m consumed per unit of process p run; defaults to zero when a process does not use a unit

params.name: k
params.doc: the available capacity of processing unit m

params.name: ur
params.doc: the maximum quantity of crude oil of type cr that may be purchased

params.name: qs
params.doc: the quality specification bound for final product cf on attribute q in direction lim; a lower entry gives a minimum required level and an upper entry gives a maximum allowed level; a direction is present only for the product and attribute pairs that are actually specified

params.name: atc
params.doc: the level of quality attribute q carried by intermediate material ci derived from crude cr; this is the attribute content per unit of that material that gets carried into a blend

params.name: pf
params.doc: the selling price per unit of final product cf

params.name: pr
params.doc: the purchase cost per unit of crude oil of type cr

params.name: pd
params.doc: the purchase cost per unit of an externally bought intermediate material

params.name: op
params.doc: the operating cost per unit of process p run

vars.name: z
vars.doc: how much of process p is run on crude cr, the process level

vars.name: x
vars.doc: how much of final product cf is sold

vars.name: u
vars.doc: how much crude oil of type cr is purchased

vars.name: ui
vars.doc: how much of intermediate material ci is purchased externally under crude cr

vars.name: w
vars.doc: how much of intermediate material ci derived from crude cr is blended into final product cf, the blending level

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: total income

vars.name: phir
vars.doc: the revenue earned from selling final products

vars.name: phip
vars.doc: the cost of input materials, both crude and externally purchased intermediates

vars.name: phiw
vars.doc: the operating cost of running the processes

constraints.name: a[cr,
constraints.intent: For each crude oil type, the refinery cannot consume more of that crude than it has available. Across all the refining processes, the net amount of crude produced or consumed by running those processes on that crude, taken together with the amount of that crude purchased, must be at least zero.

constraints.name: cd:
constraints.intent: For each crude type and each intermediate material, the supply of that intermediate must cover everything blended out of it. The amount of the intermediate produced by running the processes on that crude, plus any of it purchased from outside when that intermediate is one that can be bought externally, must be at least the total amount of that intermediate blended into the final products it is allowed to go into. For an intermediate that cannot be purchased externally, only the produced amount counts on the supply side.

constraints.name: b[m,
constraints.intent: Each processing unit has a limited capacity. For each unit, the total capacity it consumes, summed over every process across both crudes according to how much capacity each process draws from that unit, must not exceed the capacity available on that unit.

constraints.name: u[cr]
constraints.intent: For each crude oil type, the amount of that crude purchased must not exceed the maximum purchase quantity allowed for it.

constraints.name: x[cf]
constraints.intent: For each final product, the amount sold must equal the total amount blended into it from all the intermediate materials, across both crudes, that are allowed to go into that product.

constraints.name: qs['lower',
constraints.intent: Some final products must meet a minimum level on a quality attribute. For each product and attribute that carries a lower specification, the total amount of that attribute contributed by everything blended into the product must be at least the required minimum level for that product and attribute applied to the amount of the product sold.

constraints.name: qs['upper',
constraints.intent: Some final products must stay under a maximum level on a quality attribute. For each product and attribute that carries an upper specification, the total amount of that attribute contributed by everything blended into the product must not exceed the maximum allowed level for that product and attribute applied to the amount of the product sold.

constraints.name: phir
constraints.intent: Sales revenue is defined as the income from selling the final products. Set the revenue equal to the sum over all final products of each product's selling price times the amount of that product sold.

constraints.name: phip
constraints.intent: Input material cost is defined as what is paid for purchased inputs. Set it equal to the cost of buying crude, which is each crude's purchase price times the amount of that crude bought summed over the crudes, plus the cost of externally purchased intermediate materials, which is each such material's purchase price times the amount of it bought, summed over those materials and over the crudes they are bought under.

constraints.name: phiw
constraints.intent: Operating cost is defined as the cost of running the processes. Set it equal to the sum over all processes of each process's per unit operating cost times the total amount of that process run across both crudes.

query: Marketing has been courting two distributors who together would take twelve units, split however we like between regular gasoline and distillate. We're evaluating whether to sign. If the refinery has to deliver at least 12 units of regular and distillate combined, where does total income land?

query: The board is pitching a slate-balance guideline: regular gasoline should make up at least a tenth of the combined premium-plus-regular gasoline pool, so we keep a foothold in that market. What does the optimizer return for income under that rule?

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep a live presence in every product market — premium, regular, distillate, fuel oil, and fuel gas alike — by shipping at least 3 units of each final product. Plug that in and walk me through the income result.

query: Procurement is negotiating a package deal: we'd commit to taking at least 10 barrels of West Texas crude, and in exchange finance wants the total crude bill held to 90 barrels across both grades combined. Run both changes together — what's the new income?

constraints.name: cd:
constraints.intent: For each crude type and each intermediate material, the supply of that intermediate must cover everything blended out of it. The amount of the intermediate produced by running the processes on that crude, plus any of it purchased from outside when that intermediate is one that can be bought externally, must be at least the total amount of that intermediate blended into the final products it is allowed to go into. For an intermediate that cannot be purchased externally, only the produced amount counts on the supply side.

constraints.name: b[m,
constraints.intent: Each processing unit has a limited capacity. For each unit, the total capacity it consumes, summed over every process across both crudes according to how much capacity each process draws from that unit, must not exceed the capacity available on that unit.

constraints.name: u[cr]
constraints.intent: For each crude oil type, the amount of that crude purchased must not exceed the maximum purchase quantity allowed for it.

constraints.name: x[cf]
constraints.intent: For each final product, the amount sold must equal the total amount blended into it from all the intermediate materials, across both crudes, that are allowed to go into that product.

constraints.name: qs['lower',
constraints.intent: Some final products must meet a minimum level on a quality attribute. For each product and attribute that carries a lower specification, the total amount of that attribute contributed by everything blended into the product must be at least the required minimum level for that product and attribute applied to the amount of the product sold.

constraints.name: qs['upper',
constraints.intent: Some final products must stay under a maximum level on a quality attribute. For each product and attribute that carries an upper specification, the total amount of that attribute contributed by everything blended into the product must not exceed the maximum allowed level for that product and attribute applied to the amount of the product sold.

constraints.name: phir
constraints.intent: Sales revenue is defined as the income from selling the final products. Set the revenue equal to the sum over all final products of each product's selling price times the amount of that product sold.

constraints.name: phip
constraints.intent: Input material cost is defined as what is paid for purchased inputs. Set it equal to the cost of buying crude, which is each crude's purchase price times the amount of that crude bought summed over the crudes, plus the cost of externally purchased intermediate materials, which is each such material's purchase price times the amount of it bought, summed over those materials and over the crudes they are bought under.

constraints.name: phiw
constraints.intent: Operating cost is defined as the cost of running the processes. Set it equal to the sum over all processes of each process's per unit operating cost times the total amount of that process run across both crudes.

query: We have the crude allowance and the atmospheric still isn't even full, yet the plan only makes about 42 units of gasoline all-in. Shouldn't we be pushing the combined premium-plus-regular gasoline output to at least 50? What's keeping the model from making that move?

model_narrative: We run a small oil refinery. We buy crude oil of different types, run it through a set of refining processes to produce intermediate streams, optionally buy some intermediates from outside, then blend those intermediates into a handful of final products that we sell. We choose how much of each process to run on each crude, how much crude and how much external intermediate material to purchase, how to blend intermediates into each product, and how much of each product to sell. The goal is to maximize profit, which is sales revenue minus the cost of input materials minus the operating cost of the processes.

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the crude oil types that can be purchased and refined

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the refining processes that turn crude into intermediate materials

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the material components that appear in process yields, including crude itself, the intermediate streams, and the final products

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final blended products that are sold

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the intermediate blending materials produced by the processes and combined into final products

sets.name: cd
sets.doc: the subset of intermediate materials that may also be bought externally rather than only produced in house; this is a subset of the intermediate materials

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the capacity-limited processing units whose throughput is bounded

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the measurable quality attributes of a blended product, such as octane and sulfur content

sets.name: lim
sets.doc: the two directions a quality specification can take, a lower bound or an upper bound

sets.name: bp
sets.doc: the set of allowed pairs of a final product and an intermediate material; a pair belongs to this set exactly when that intermediate is permitted to be blended into that final product

params.name: a
params.doc: the yield coefficient of component c from crude cr in process p; a positive value means the process produces that component and a negative value means it consumes it, per unit of process run; defaults to zero when a component is not involved in a process

params.name: b
params.doc: the amount of capacity of processing unit m consumed per unit of process p run; defaults to zero when a process does not use a unit

params.name: k
params.doc: the available capacity of processing unit m

params.name: ur
params.doc: the maximum quantity of crude oil of type cr that may be purchased

params.name: qs
params.doc: the quality specification bound for final product cf on attribute q in direction lim; a lower entry gives a minimum required level and an upper entry gives a maximum allowed level; a direction is present only for the product and attribute pairs that are actually specified

params.name: atc
params.doc: the level of quality attribute q carried by intermediate material ci derived from crude cr; this is the attribute content per unit of that material that gets carried into a blend

params.name: pf
params.doc: the selling price per unit of final product cf

params.name: pr
params.doc: the purchase cost per unit of crude oil of type cr

params.name: pd
params.doc: the purchase cost per unit of an externally bought intermediate material

params.name: op
params.doc: the operating cost per unit of process p run

vars.name: z
vars.doc: how much of process p is run on crude cr, the process level

vars.name: x
vars.doc: how much of final product cf is sold

vars.name: u
vars.doc: how much crude oil of type cr is purchased

vars.name: ui
vars.doc: how much of intermediate material ci is purchased externally under crude cr

vars.name: w
vars.doc: how much of intermediate material ci derived from crude cr is blended into final product cf, the blending level

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: total income

vars.name: phir
vars.doc: the revenue earned from selling final products

vars.name: phip
vars.doc: the cost of input materials, both crude and externally purchased intermediates

vars.name: phiw
vars.doc: the operating cost of running the processes

query: Nearly half of everything we ship is premium gasoline — one product wobble and the whole quarter is at risk. Couldn't we hold premium to at most 45 percent of all five final products combined and stay diversified? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
expected_pyomo: model.premium_slate_cap = Constraint(expr=model.x['premium'] <= 0.45 * sum(model.x[cf] for cf in model.cf))

query: Reformate is our octane workhorse, yet the premium blend leans heavily on straight-run gas. Shouldn't every gasoline blend — premium and regular both — draw at least 45 percent of its volume from reformate? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: More than half our slate goes out the door as fuel oil and fuel gas — our two cheapest products. Wouldn't it be smarter to cap fuel oil plus fuel gas at 40 units combined and stop feeding the low end of the barrel? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
